<email>
From: nicole.harris@gmail.com
To: support@seatsafe.app
Subject: Just switched to SeatSafe from CarSeatPro
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T16:30:00
Body: Hi SeatSafe team,

I'm Nicole — just set up my account. I want to give you some context on why I switched, because I think it'll help you understand what I'm looking for.

I was using CarSeatPro for the past six months for car seat safety assessments. I deleted it three weeks ago. That app was absolutely useless — it flagged every single car seat I looked at with some kind of warning. It treated a perfectly safe, NHTSA-rated seat like it was a death trap.

The worst part was the money I wasted. I'd get a "safety concern" flag, panic, and either return a car seat I'd already bought or pay extra for a "recommended alternative" that turned out to be functionally identical. I returned a $280 Graco convertible because CarSeatPro flagged it over a theoretical heat tolerance issue — something about EPS foam softening at extreme temperatures. I took it to a certified car seat technician at our local fire station and she literally laughed. She said she's installed thousands of that exact model across the Phoenix metro area and has never seen a heat-related failure. That was $45 in restocking fees plus two weeks without a proper car seat for my toddler while I waited for the replacement to ship.

Then it flagged a Chicco infant seat because the crash test data was "more than 4 years old." I called Chicco directly — they told me the seat design hasn't changed and the crash test results are still valid. Another $30 in return shipping for nothing.

Between restocking fees, return shipping, and the "upgraded" seats CarSeatPro pushed me toward, I wasted easily $600 in four months. My husband was furious. He said I was letting an app make me paranoid about car seats that millions of parents use safely every day.

I'm giving SeatSafe a shot because my sister-in-law Jen swears by it. But if this app is going to flag everything with theoretical edge cases like CarSeatPro did, I'd rather just ask the car seat technician at the fire station directly.

Nicole
</email>

<email>
From: nicole.harris@gmail.com
To: support@seatsafe.app
Subject: One more thing about why I left CarSeatPro
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T17:15:22
Body: Hi again,

Sorry, one more thing — I was talking to my sister-in-law Jen about this and she made a really good point. She said the problem with car seat safety apps is they're built to be maximally cautious because the companies are terrified of liability. So they flag everything that could theoretically be an issue, even when the actual real-world risk is negligible.

Jen has three kids and has been through probably a dozen car seats over the past eight years. She said most of what these apps flag falls into "technically possible but practically irrelevant" territory — things that sound alarming in a spec sheet but that no certified car seat technician would actually worry about. She said the key is practical calibration: understanding the difference between a genuine safety deficiency and a theoretical concern that doesn't translate to real risk.

I thought that was really well put. Not everything that sounds concerning in a technical document IS concerning in practice. Context matters — a crash test being five years old could mean the seat is outdated, or it could mean the design hasn't changed and the results are still perfectly valid. I need an app that can tell the difference instead of treating both situations the same way.

Just wanted to share that perspective so you have some context for how I think about car seat safety.

- Nicole
</email>
